text: The surgeon measures the span between the umbilicus and pubic bone using eight fingers, indicating the placement of ports for the procedure. A small incision is made at the umbilicus for port insertion, with secondary ports positioned in the mid-clavicular line at the horizontal level of the umbilicus. The surgeon uses artery forceps to open the umbilicus, ensuring entry into the correct plane.

The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and performs the plunger test by aspirating with a syringe filled with saline. The absence of blood or bile confirms entry into the peritoneal cavity. Saline flows freely, indicating successful cavity access. The gas is then connected, with the flow rate and pressure gradually increasing to ensure safe insufflation.

The surgeon uses a bladed trocar through the XL port, inserting a zero-degree, ten mm scope. As the scope advances, the layers of skin, subcutaneous tissue, anterior rectal sheath, ulterior rectal sheath, and preperitoneal fat are sequentially visualized, culminating in the opening of the peritoneal cavity, referred to as the pupillary sign.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the anatomy to ensure no inadvertent injury has occurred. The ileopubic tract is identified, along with the pubic ramus and pubic bone. A horizontal line is drawn from the anterior superior iliac spine to the pubis, marking the ileopubic tract. Anterior to this line, the area is divided by the inferior epigastric vessels, consisting of two veins and one artery. The surgeon notes that a direct hernia defect would be medial to these vessels.

The surgeon identifies the medial umbilical ligament, cautioning not to cross it to avoid bladder injury. The triangle of doom is visible, showing the right-sided vas deferens turning medially. Testicular vessels are observed going laterally. The blue structure is the right external iliac vein, medially placed, and the white structure is the right external iliac artery, laterally placed.

The camera view shows the angle of doom formed by the vas deferens and testicular vessels. The surgeon manipulates the peritoneum to convert this angle into a triangle of doom, with the reflected peritoneum forming the third limb. The psoas muscle is visible, along with the genitofemoral nerve, which divides into the genital and femoral branches. The iliacus muscle is also visible, with the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh running over it.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, direct hernia defect, iliopubic tract, medial umbilical ligament, vas deferens on the left side, and testicular vessels on the lateral side, forming the angle and triangle of doom. An incision is made approximately two centimeters anterior to the anterior superior iliac spine, with carbon dioxide insufflation creating space in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a trans-abdominal pre-peritoneal hernia repair, using a harmonic scalpel and hunter-grasper to dissect close to the peritoneum. The fascia transversalis is pushed upwards, revealing the anterior abdominal wall fat. A 30-degree, 10mm scope provides visualization as the surgeon maintains a preperitoneal plane, ensuring the dissection remains snug with the peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the peritoneum and fascia transversalis to minimize bleeding, revealing the mobilized peritoneum. The right external artery is visible, along with the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve. As the dissection progresses laterally, the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh is exposed, ensuring no injury or neuropraxia occurs during the procedure.

The surgeon has created a marsupial space for mesh placement, ensuring it remains unfolded. The dissection crosses the inferior epigastric vessels, revealing the external iliac artery laterally and the inferior epigastric veins draining into the medially placed external iliac vein. The fascia is carefully dissected away from the anterior abdominal wall to avoid exposing muscle fibers, while pulling the sac up to prevent injury to the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon carefully retracts the pseudo sac on the scrotal side, minimizing bleeding by maintaining close proximity to the peritoneum. The pseudo sac is pushed and pulled to ensure the mesh will be placed without rolling at the inferior margin, while the peritoneal lining is taken into the abdomen.

The surgeon carefully separates the pseudo sac from the fascia transversalis using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, ensuring no injury to the inferior epigastric vessels. The dissection avoids rubbing against the pubic ramus to prevent bleeding from the vessels of the corona mortis, which are visible as branches of the external iliac artery and vein.

The surgeon creates space on the medial side, staying close to the peritoneum and avoiding the fascia transversalis, which is directed towards the rectus muscle. The fascia transversalis is seen moving onto the rectus muscle. The surgeon identifies the 'white house' or 'lighthouse' landmark. Any buttonhole defects are noted for suturing at the end of the procedure. The vas deferens is carefully separated from the surrounding vessels, a process referred to as peritilization, to avoid mishandling.

The surgeon carefully retracts the horizontal limb of the peritoneum, revealing the testicular vessels and the vas deferens turning medially. The vas deferens is not handled directly, maintaining its integrity while the peritoneal sac or pseudo sac is separated and moved towards the peritoneal cavity to ensure proper mesh placement without folding or rolling.

The camera is rotated to reveal the pubic ramus and loose areolar tissue, which is being removed. The surgeon mobilizes the superior lip of the peritoneum to create a marsupial space for a 15x12 mesh, ensuring careful navigation around the retro pubic cave of Radzias. The anatomy is reviewed, showing the medial umbilical ligament and the direct defect up to its fundus. The external iliac vein is visible, showing pulsations from the external iliac artery, while the peritoneum is mobilized.

The surgeon forms the triangle of dome by carefully adhering to the peritoneum during the TAPP procedure. The right external iliac artery and vein are visible, and dissection has reached the mid belly of the psoas muscle. The superior margin of the mesh is unveiled, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessels to prevent injury. The posterior and anterior lamina of the fascia transversalis are visible, emphasizing the importance of respecting the peritoneum and fascia transversalis.

The surgeon proceeds to the contralateral side, performing dissection from medial to lateral while staying close to the peritoneum. The medial umbilical ligament is identified, and care is taken not to cross it. The pseudo sac is located, and the surgeon ensures that the round ligament is cut in cases of female inguinal hernia repair, particularly for elderly women. The fascia transversalis is carefully managed during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lip of the peritoneum, ensuring it is not left behind to prevent recurrence. The dissection remains close to the peritoneum to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels, testicular vessels, and vas deferens. The surgeon repeatedly checks the area to prevent button holding in the peritoneum.

In the open hernia surgery, the surgeon leaves the distal sac open to prevent hydrocele formation, avoiding excessive dissection of the indirect sac to minimize the risk of seroma and injury to the testicular vessels. The surgeon carefully respects the vas deferens and vessels, ensuring they are not pulled or manhandled to prevent micro damage. The external iliac vein and artery are visible, and the peritoneum is being closed.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum with sutures in a thin patient to minimize post-operative pain and avoid nerve transgression. The external iliac vein is visible medially, and the iliopubic tract is identified. The surgeon ensures not to go medial to avoid the areas where femoral or obturator hernias could occur.

The surgeon carefully dissects the horizontal limb of the peritoneum, ensuring the testicular vessels are positioned laterally and the vas deferens turns medially, marking the limits of dissection. The peritoneum is held close to avoid deep tissue injury, with the vas deferens visible turning medially. The dissection reaches the area of the cave of Redzius, and the superior limit is established for mesh placement, avoiding undulations.

The surgeon prepares to close the peritoneum after placing a 15x15 cm proline mesh. The mesh is carefully handled, with all staff members changing gloves to minimize infection risk. The mesh's inferior margin is visibly thicker and company-designed, and it is cut to fit the surgical site.

The surgeon places the mesh on the superior aspect of the surgical field, ensuring it does not curl due to its pre-formed structure. The mesh is fixed with sutures at specific junctions: one at the junction of one-third and two-thirds medially, and another at the junction of lateral one-third and two-thirds. The mesh is folded such that approximately six centimeters are folded in, while six centimeters remain unfolded.

The surgeon positions a heavyweight proline mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, onto the right side through a twelve mm port. The mesh is carefully placed with its inferior margin approximately five centimeters away from the defect, ensuring it rests comfortably in the marsupial space. The medial aspect of the mesh is cut and prepared separately for the right side deployment.

The surgeon positions the mesh to overlap the midline, ensuring it rests comfortably over the bladder area. Using Covidien 5mm tags, the mesh is secured to Cooper's ligament without excessive force to avoid penetrating bone and causing osteitis. The surgical field is clear of vessels on Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon carefully places the mesh, ensuring the inferior margin rests comfortably without applying excessive force. The mesh is positioned above the iliopubic tract to avoid the triangle of pain, and sutures are used to secure it in place. The mesh is opened onto the roof in the TAPP approach.

The surgeon places a tack onto the rectus muscle, ensuring it covers the defect by approximately five centimeters. The mesh is positioned on the lateral aspect near the anterior superior iliac spine, with care taken to avoid excessive pressure due to the patient's thin build. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, and the mesh crosses the midline as another mesh is prepared for placement on the left side.

The surgeon places two tags on the Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh, ensuring it covers the direct, indirect, femoral, and obturator hernias on one side. The mesh is positioned to avoid excessive pressure on the bone, preventing potential damage. The inferior margin of the mesh is checked to ensure it does not fold, which could lead to hernia recurrence. The mesh is unfurled up to the area of the bladder, resting comfortably within the marsupialized space.

The surgeon ensures a good overlap of the mesh on the medial side and places a tack to secure both meshes, preventing slippage. The mesh is stretched and pressure is applied from above for fixation without penetrating too deeply into the rectus muscle. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, and care is taken not to tack them. The surgeon is prepared to manage potential bleeding by understanding the anatomy and using appropriate techniques.

The surgeon places the mesh on both sides and begins suturing on the right side, followed by the left. Care is taken to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels during suturing. The needle is pulled in the same direction as insertion to facilitate opposing the peritoneum, which has been extensively dissected.

The surgeon carefully avoids injury to the inferior epigastric vessels while managing the redundant peritoneum. The peritoneum is utilized to prevent bleeding, ensuring no need for suction irrigation. The mesh is handled with care to avoid contamination, emphasizing the importance of preventing infection by not leaving blood or saline inside the surgical site.
